checking-special-cases-first.label: Checking Special Cases First
checking-special-cases-first.summary: Use clear early checks to make conditional logic easier to read.

sketch1.title: Put specific checks before broad checks

code_zero_before_small.title: Check zero before a wider range

code_invalid_code_first.title: Validate a code before normal cases

sc-special-before-general.title: Choose the best ordering
sc-special-before-general.prompt: Why is it usually better to check a special case before a broad case in an `if` / `elif` chain?
sc-special-before-general.hint: Python uses the first branch whose condition is true.

help.concept: In an `if` / `elif` chain, Python checks conditions from top to bottom and stops at the first true one. A broad condition can hide a more specific case if it appears first.
help.hint_1: Think about what happens when one value matches both a specific condition and a wider condition.
help.hint_2: If the wider condition is first, Python will take that branch and skip the later special-case branch.

options.a: Because Python runs every matching branch in order
options.b: Because a broad condition placed first can prevent the special case from being reached
options.d: Because special cases must always be written in `else`

sc-output-perfect-score.title: Predict the output
sc-output-perfect-score.hint: Check which condition becomes true first.

help.concept: Python evaluates `if` and `elif` branches in order. Even if a later condition is also true, it will not run once an earlier true branch is found.
help.hint_1: Ask whether `100 >= 60` is true before thinking about the `elif` line.
help.hint_2: Once the first branch matches, Python prints from that branch and skips the rest.

options.d: It prints both Pass and Perfect

mc-good-special-case-order.title: Find the good examples
mc-good-special-case-order.prompt: Which snippets check a special case before a broader case? Choose all that apply.
mc-good-special-case-order.hint: Look for a narrow condition placed above a wider one.

help.concept: A special case is a narrower condition, such as `score == 100`, while a broad case covers more values, such as `score >= 60`. Good ordering puts the narrower case first.
help.hint_1: Check whether the first condition is a subset of a later condition or the other way around.
help.hint_2: A good answer handles the exact edge case before the condition that would also include it.

mc-values-hit-first-branch.title: Which values take the first branch?
mc-values-hit-first-branch.hint: Use the lesson explanation and the wording of this question to narrow the answer.

dr-order-score-checks.title: Order the branches
dr-order-score-checks.prompt: Arrange these conditions from best to worst order for a grading check where `100` should get a special message before normal passing scores.
dr-order-score-checks.hint: Put the most specific condition first.

help.concept: When one condition is more specific than another, the specific one should come earlier so it is not hidden by a broader match.
help.hint_1: An exact equality check for `100` is narrower than a pass-range check.
help.hint_2: The catch-all branch belongs last because it handles everything not matched earlier.

dr-order-input-validation.title: Order validation logic
dr-order-input-validation.prompt: Arrange these checks in a clear order for an age classifier. Invalid ages should be handled before normal ages.
dr-order-input-validation.hint: Start with the impossible value range.

help.concept: Validation often works best when invalid or exceptional inputs are checked first, then special valid cases, then the general case.
help.hint_1: A negative age is invalid, so it should be checked before any normal age category.
help.hint_2: After invalid input, place the exact edge case before the broad remaining case.

fb-empty-string-check.title: Complete the special-case check
fb-empty-string-check.prompt: Fill in the missing value so the code checks for an empty string first.
fb-empty-string-check.hint: An empty string has no characters.

help.concept: In Python, an empty string is written with two quotes and nothing between them. It is a common special case to check before other text values.
help.hint_1: You need the string literal that contains zero characters.
help.hint_2: Look for the choice that is just opening and closing quotes with nothing inside.

fb-elif-keyword.title: Choose the right keyword
fb-elif-keyword.prompt: Fill in the missing keyword to check a second condition only if the first one was false.

help.concept: Use `the missing term` in Python when you want another condition checked only if earlier conditions in the same chain were false.
help.hint_1: It is not `if`, because that would start a separate condition block.
help.hint_2: It is the keyword used between `if` and `else` in a multi-branch chain.

code-invalid-before-range.title: Check invalid input first
code-invalid-before-range.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer age from input. If the age is less than 0, print `Invalid`. Otherwise, if the age is less than 13, print `Child`. In all other cases, print `Older`.
code-invalid-before-range.hint: Handle the impossible age before the normal age groups.

help.concept: When one input is invalid or exceptional, checking it first keeps the rest of the branching logic simpler and clearer.
help.hint_1: Read the age with `input()`, convert it to `int`, then test `age < 0` before the other conditions.
help.hint_2: After the invalid check, the next branch should cover ages from 0 up to 12, and the final branch handles the rest.

code-invalid-before-range.starterCode:
age = int(input())

# TODO: print Invalid, Child, or Older

code-zero-before-small.title: Check zero before a wider range
code-zero-before-small.prompt: Write Python code that reads one integer `n`. If `n` is exactly 0, print `Zero`. Otherwise, if `n` is less than or equal to 10, print `Small`. In all other cases, print `Large`.
code-zero-before-small.hint: The exact value 0 belongs to the wider `<= 10` range, so order matters.

help.concept: When a special value is included inside a broader range, the exact-value check must come first or it will be hidden by the range check.
help.hint_1: Read the number, then compare with `== 0` before using `<= 10`.
help.hint_2: The second branch should catch values like 1 and 10, while the final branch handles numbers above 10.

n = int(input())

# TODO: print Zero, Small, or Large

special-cases-come-first.title: Why check special cases first?
special-cases-come-first.bodyMarkdown:
When a program has several possible paths, it often helps to check the unusual or important cases first. This makes your logic easier to read because the exceptions are handled early, and the more ordinary case can come later.

For example, imagine a ticket checker:

```python
age = 0

if age < 0:
    print("Invalid age")
elif age == 0:
    print("Newborn ticket")
else:
    print("Regular age group")
```

Step by step:
- `if age < 0:` checks an invalid input first.
- `elif age == 0:` checks a special edge case next.
- `else:` handles everything left over.

This order is useful because invalid or rare values can be separated before the normal path.

If you put the broad case too early, later checks may never run.

**Try it yourself:** Type the code in the code editor, change `age` to `-2`, `0`, and `5`, click Run, and compare the output panel each time.

broad-vs-specific.title: Put specific checks before broad checks
broad-vs-specific.bodyMarkdown:
A common mistake is writing a broad condition before a more specific one. Python stops at the first matching branch, so order matters.

Look at this example:

```python
score = 100

if score >= 60:
    print("Pass")
elif score == 100:
    print("Perfect score")
else:
    print("Needs work")
```

Here, `score == 100` is a special case, but it comes after `score >= 60`. Since `100` is also greater than or equal to `60`, Python prints `Pass` and never reaches `Perfect score`.

A clearer version is:

```python
score = 100

if score == 100:
    print("Perfect score")
elif score >= 60:
    print("Pass")
else:
    print("Needs work")
```

Now the special case is checked first.

**Worked example:**
- If `score` is `100`, output is `Perfect score`.
- If `score` is `75`, output is `Pass`.
- If `score` is `40`, output is `Needs work`.

**Your turn:** Change the value of `score` and run the code to see which branch is chosen.

Line by line: read each statement from top to bottom. Notice which names are created, which values are passed into functions, and what output the code is meant to show.

using-elif-for-clear-branches.title: Use `elif` to separate cases clearly
using-elif-for-clear-branches.bodyMarkdown:
When only one branch should run, `if` / `elif` / `else` is a good pattern. It lets you list cases in a clear order from most special to most general.

Example:

```python
text = ""

if text == "":
    print("Empty string")
elif text == "yes":
    print("Confirmed")
else:
    print("Other text")
```

Line by line:
- `text == ""` checks the special case of an empty string.
- `text == "yes"` checks a specific meaningful value.
- `else` catches every other string.

This is easier to understand than mixing broad and narrow checks in the wrong order.

**Try this:** Replace `text` with `"yes"`, `"hello"`, and `""`. Click Run and check the output panel after each change.
